results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly restricts eligible applicants to Troop- and Police-Contributing Countries (T/PCCs) and UN organisations. While the ILO is a UN agency, the eligible applicant categories are specifically defined as T/PCCs (member states contributing troops/police to peacekeeping) and UN organisations within the context of peacekeeping operations; the ILO does not fit the operational profile of eligible UN entities described. Additionally, the deadline for the first mandatory submission step (LOI by 31 January 2026) has already passed as of today (2026-04-23).
results.classification_summary: The Elsie Initiative Fund Fourth Programming Round is ineligible for the ILO on two decisive grounds. First, the eligible applicant categories are strictly limited to Troop- and Police-Contributing Countries (T/PCCs) and UN organisations operating in the peacekeeping context; the ILO does not fit this profile. Second, the mandatory LOI submission deadline of 31 January 2026 has passed, rendering the opportunity closed.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The Concept Note explicitly states: 'Eligibility Criteria: T/PCCs and UN organisations. See EIF Terms of Reference, section 4.2.5.' The call is directed at troop/police-contributing countries and UN peacekeeping-related entities. The ILO does not fall within the T/PCC category and, while technically a UN-affiliated body, is not a UN agency, fund or programme engaged in peacekeeping operations as described.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The EIF provides non-reimbursable financial and technical support (Barrier Assessment Funding, Flexible Project Funding) to eligible entities. The Gender Strong Unit Premium is described as a reimbursement upon deployment, but the primary modalities include grant-type funding.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No specific minimum or maximum grant amount per project is stated in the available documentation.

Deadline is future.evidence: The LOI submission deadline was 31 January 2026 (extended to 31 March 2026 for UN missions). Both dates have passed as of today, 2026-04-23.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a programming round call for Letters of Interest and project proposals, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing or cost-sharing requirement is mentioned in the call documentation.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required; eligibility is based on being a T/PCC or UN organisation.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium or partnership leadership rules are described in the call documentation.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Eligibility is not restricted to applicants from any specific donor country; T/PCCs from any member state and relevant UN organisations are eligible.

results.title: Elsie Initiative Fund – Fourth Programming Round Call for Letters of Interest
results.deadline_text: LOIs must be submitted to elsie.mptf@unwomen.org by 31 January 2026 (extended to 31 March 2026 for UN peacekeeping missions)
results.organization: Government of Canada (Global Affairs Canada) and multi-donor contributors via the Elsie Initiative Fund hosted by UN Women
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Submission of Letter of Interest (LOI) – general deadline for T/PCCs and UN organisations
Extended LOI submission deadline for UN peacekeeping missions

eligible_activities: Barrier assessment funding: national barrier assessments using MOWIP or equivalent standardised methodology
Flexible project funding: policy development, leadership initiatives, gender-sensitive training, infrastructure improvements based on credible barrier assessment
Gender Strong Unit (GSU) Premium: reimbursement upon successful deployment of a verified gender-strong unit
funding_restrictions: Only T/PCCs and UN organisations are eligible
Flexible Project Funding requires prior credible barrier assessment results
GSU Premium requires independent verification of the gender-strong unit deployment
results.proposal_summary: The Elsie Initiative Fund (EIF), a multi-donor global fund hosted by UN Women and established by the UN, Canada, and Member States, launched its Fourth Programming Round on 9 October 2025. The Fund supports institutional reforms to increase the full, equal, and meaningful participation of uniformed women in UN peace operations. Eligible applicants are Troop- and Police-Contributing Countries (T/PCCs) and UN organisations, including UN peacekeeping missions. Funding modalities include Barrier Assessment Funding, Flexible Project Funding for targeted institutional reforms, and a Gender Strong Unit (GSU) Premium. No specific per-project funding ceiling is publicly stated. LOIs were due by 31 January 2026 for T/PCCs and UN organisations, and by 31 March 2026 for UN peacekeeping missions. The ILO is not an eligible applicant under this call.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on gender equality and institutional reform within UN peacekeeping security institutions, which has limited overlap with the ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO (a UN agency) is explicitly excluded from eligibility. The CfP states that 'UN agencies or UN Country Teams' are NOT eligible to apply for a grant. The application deadline of 20 October 2025 has also passed.
results.classification_summary: The CfP explicitly lists 'UN agencies or UN Country Teams' among ineligible applicants, directly excluding the ILO. Additionally, the application deadline of 20 October 2025 has passed as of today's date. Both criteria independently render this opportunity ineligible for the ILO.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP document (Section 4.1) explicitly states that the following are NOT eligible: 'UN agencies or UN Country Teams'. The ILO, as a UN agency, is directly excluded.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call explicitly offers grants to civil society organizations: programmatic funding from USD 30,000 to USD 200,000 and institutional funding from USD 2,500 to USD 30,000 as non-reimbursable grants.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Programmatic funding ranges from USD 30,000 to USD 200,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The application deadline was 20 October 2025, which is in the past relative to today's date of 2026-04-23. The page itself is titled 'Closed: Call for Proposals in Syria'.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for proposals for grants to civil society organizations, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Section 4.5 explicitly states: 'NO. Civil society organizations are not required to contribute to the budget.'

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required. Organizations need legal registration in the eligible country.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The CfP allows joint projects led by local women's rights or women-led CSOs. There is no explicit rule requiring consortium leadership by a non-UN entity beyond the general ineligibility of UN agencies as applicants.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The CfP is restricted to national, regional or local/grassroots organizations legally registered in Syria. International NGOs are explicitly excluded, and the lead applicant must have legal status with the competent national authority in the eligible country.

Enhanced protection, safety, security and mental health of women and girls (Impact Area 5)
Improved socio-economic recovery and political participation of women and young women in peacebuilding contexts, targeting forcibly displaced women (Impact Area 6)
Institutional capacity building of women's rights/led organizations (institutional funding stream)
Women-led advocacy campaigns and community dialogues

results.proposal_summary: The Women's Peace and Humanitarian Fund (WPHF), a UN and civil society partnership managed with UNDP and UNFPA as management entities, launched a Call for Proposals in Syria (now closed) to provide grants to local women's rights and women-led civil society organizations. The call targeted organizations implementing projects in Syria focused on humanitarian and crisis response, protection of women and girls, and peacebuilding and recovery, with special emphasis on forcibly displaced women. Programmatic funding ranged from USD 30,000 to USD 200,000, while institutional funding ranged from USD 2,500 to USD 30,000. Eligible applicants were exclusively national, regional, or local/grassroots organizations legally registered in Syria; UN agencies, international NGOs, governments, and private entities were explicitly excluded. The application deadline was 20 October 2025, and implementation was to run until 30 September 2026 (Impact Areas 3 & 5) or a maximum of 24 months (Impact Area 6). Co-financing was not required.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CfP touches on socio-economic recovery and women's empowerment in crisis contexts that are adjacent to ILO themes such as decent work and social protection, its primary focus is on humanitarian response, protection of women and girls, and peacebuilding rather than core ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, or social dialogue.

results.exclusion_reason: Eligible applicants are restricted to local civil society organizations (CSOs) in Sudan; the ILO as an international/UN organization does not fit any listed eligible category. Additionally, the submission deadline of 21 October 2025 has passed.
results.classification_summary: This call is ineligible for the ILO on two grounds. First, the CfP explicitly targets local civil society organizations in Sudan, and the ILO as an international/UN organization falls outside the exhaustive list of eligible applicants. Second, the submission deadline of 21 October 2025 has passed as of today's date.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP states WPHF seeks to fund 'qualifying local organizations in Sudan' through programmatic and institutional funding streams aimed at CSOs/women's rights organizations. The ILO, as a UN/international organization, is not listed among eligible applicant categories.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CfP provides non-reimbursable programmatic funding (USD 30,000–200,000) and institutional funding (USD 2,500–30,000) to civil society organizations — these are grants.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Programmatic funding ranges from USD 30,000 to USD 200,000, which exceeds the USD 50,000 threshold. (Institutional funding of USD 2,500–30,000 is below threshold, but the programmatic stream is above.)

Deadline is future.evidence: The stated deadline is 21 October 2025 at 11:59pm Geneva CET, which is in the past relative to today's date of 2026-04-23. The page also notes 'This call for Proposal is Now Closed'.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The opportunity is framed as a Call for Proposals for grant funding to CSOs, not a procurement/tender for goods or services.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing or cost-sharing requirement is mentioned in the CfP text or supporting documents.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required; applicants need to be qualifying local organizations in Sudan.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the CfP text.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The CfP explicitly targets 'qualifying local organizations in Sudan,' restricting eligibility to locally registered/based organizations in Sudan.

results.proposal_summary: The Women's Peace and Humanitarian Fund (WPHF), a UN and civil society partnership administered through UN Women, launched a Call for Proposals (CfP6) in Sudan to support local civil society organizations (CSOs) through two funding streams: programmatic funding (USD 30,000–200,000) for activities related to women's participation in humanitarian planning/crisis response or protection of women and girls from SGBV, and institutional funding (USD 2,500–30,000) to reinforce the capacity of women's rights and women-led organizations. Eligible applicants are qualifying local organizations in Sudan; international organizations and UN agencies are not included in the eligible categories. Applications were submitted online or by email to WPHFapplications@unwomen.org with the subject line 'Sudan CfP6,' with a deadline of 21 October 2025 (now closed). No co-financing requirement was specified, and implementation period details were not stated in the available documents.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CfP addresses humanitarian crisis response and protection of women and girls in Sudan, its focus on gender-based violence, women's participation in peace processes, and institutional CSO capacity does not closely align with the ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, or social dialogue.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly excludes UN agencies from applying. Deadline is future: The deadline of 31 October 2025 has passed (today is 2026-04-23).
results.classification_summary: The WPHF Lebanon Call for Proposals explicitly lists UN agencies as ineligible applicants, directly excluding the ILO. The submission deadline of 31 October 2025 has also passed. Both hard exclusion criteria fail, making this opportunity ineligible for ILO.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP document explicitly states that 'UN agencies or UN Country Teams' are NOT eligible to apply for a grant from WPHF. The ILO, as a UN agency, is therefore excluded.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call provides non-reimbursable grants ranging from USD 2,500 to USD 300,000 to civil society organizations.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Programmatic funding ranges from USD 30,000 to USD 300,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold. Institutional funding ranges from USD 2,500 to USD 30,000, which is below threshold, but the programmatic stream exceeds it.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline was 31 October 2025 at 11:59pm Geneva CET, which has passed as of today (2026-04-23). The call page is also marked as 'Closed'.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant call for proposals, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The CfP explicitly states 'Civil society organizations are not required to contribute to the budget.'

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required. Only legal registration in Lebanon is required for the lead applicant.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: Joint projects are encouraged, but there is no rule requiring the consortium to be led by a non-UN entity specifically; the restriction applies to all UN agencies at the eligibility level.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Only national, regional, or local/grassroots organizations legally registered in Lebanon are eligible. International NGOs are explicitly excluded.

results.proposal_summary: The Women's Peace and Humanitarian Fund (WPHF) launched its sixth Call for Proposals in Lebanon (September–October 2025), offering grants to local civil society organizations focused on humanitarian and crisis response, protection of women and girls, and peacebuilding and recovery. Programmatic grants range from USD 30,000 to USD 300,000 (Impact Areas 3 and 5) and from USD 30,000 to USD 150,000 (Impact Area 6, targeting forcibly displaced women), while institutional/core funding ranges from USD 2,500 to USD 30,000. Eligible applicants are national, regional, or local women's rights, women-led, youth-focused, or feminist civil society organizations legally registered in Lebanon; UN agencies, international NGOs, government bodies, private entities, and universities are explicitly excluded. Projects can run for up to 24 months. The submission deadline was 31 October 2025 and the call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call addresses crisis response and women's participation — areas adjacent to ILO's decent work agenda — its focus is on humanitarian protection, peacebuilding, and women's rights in Lebanon rather than core ILO themes such as labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or social dialogue, and the ILO is explicitly excluded as a UN agency.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline has passed: the submission deadline was 31 January 2024, which is before today's date of 2026-04-23. Additionally, eligible applicants are restricted to local civil society organizations (CSOs) in Haiti, explicitly excluding international organizations such as the ILO.
results.classification_summary: This call is ineligible on two grounds. First, the submission deadline of 31 January 2024 has long passed. Second, the eligible applicant pool is explicitly restricted to local civil society organizations (women's and young women's organizations) in Haiti, which excludes the ILO as an international organization. The grant is non-reimbursable and ranges from USD 2,500 to USD 200,000, but these merits are overridden by the deadline and eligibility failures.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP explicitly targets local civil society organizations (OSC) in Haiti — specifically local women's and young women's organizations. The ILO, as a UN/international organization, does not fall within this exhaustive category of eligible applicants.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call offers programmatic funding (USD 30,000–200,000) and institutional funding (USD 2,500–30,000) as non-reimbursable grants to CSOs.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Programmatic funding goes up to USD 200,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold. Even institutional funding up to USD 30,000 is below threshold, but the programmatic window exceeds it.

Deadline is future.evidence: The stated deadline is 31 January 2024, which has passed relative to today's date of 2026-04-23. The page is also marked 'Closed'.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is framed as a grant call for proposals for CSOs, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing requirement is mentioned in the CfP documents.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required; eligibility is based on being a local CSO in Haiti working in the relevant areas.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the available documents.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is explicitly restricted to local civil society organizations based and operating in Haiti.

results.title: WPHF Call for Proposals in Haiti – Women's Leadership in Humanitarian Response, Protection, and Socio-Economic Recovery
results.deadline_text: DATE LIMITE DE CANDIDATURE : 31 janvier 2024
results.organization: Women's Peace and Humanitarian Fund (WPHF) / UN Women
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Online information session for potential applicants
Submission deadline for complete application dossiers

eligible_activities: Promoting meaningful participation and decision-making of women in humanitarian and crisis response (Impact Area 3)
Strengthening protection of women and girls, including GBV response and prevention (Impact Area 5)
Supporting socio-economic recovery and political participation of women and young women in peacebuilding contexts (Impact Area 6)
Institutional capacity strengthening of local CSOs working on the Women, Peace and Security agenda (Institutional Funding)
funding_restrictions: Funding is restricted to local civil society organizations in Haiti
International organizations and non-local entities are not eligible
Institutional funding is limited to USD 2,500–30,000 and restricted to organizational capacity strengthening
results.proposal_summary: The Women's Peace and Humanitarian Fund (WPHF), managed through UN Women, launched a call for proposals for local civil society organizations (including women's and young women's organizations) in Haiti. The call aimed to increase women's participation and leadership in humanitarian response, strengthen protection of women and girls, and support socio-economic recovery in peacebuilding contexts. Two funding modalities were offered: programmatic funding (USD 30,000–200,000) for project activities under three impact areas, and institutional funding (USD 2,500–30,000) for organizational capacity strengthening. The call was restricted to locally registered CSOs in Haiti, and the submission deadline was 31 January 2024. No co-financing was required. The call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call addresses humanitarian action, women's protection, and socio-economic recovery — themes partially aligned with ILO's decent work and crisis response mandates — it is exclusively targeting local Haitian CSOs and focuses on gender-based violence and women's peace and security rather than core ILO labour mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO (as a non-governmental, international organization) is explicitly excluded from eligibility. Only governments, sub-national governmental entities, and regional intergovernmental entities from ODA-eligible countries may apply. Additionally, the application deadline of 7 April 2025 has passed.
results.classification_summary: The Nature for Health Phase II Expression of Interest is exclusively open to governments, sub-national governmental entities, and regional intergovernmental entities from OECD DAC ODA-eligible countries; non-governmental entities including the ILO are explicitly excluded. The application deadline of 7 April 2025 has passed as of today's date (2026-04-23), rendering the call closed. Both the eligibility restriction and the expired deadline independently disqualify the ILO from this opportunity.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The EOI explicitly states: 'The EOI is only open for submissions from governments, including sub-national governmental entities, as well as regional intergovernmental entities that represent multiple governments. Non-governmental entities are not eligible to apply.' The ILO, as a UN/intergovernmental agency rather than a government or regional intergovernmental entity representing member states in this context, does not fit the eligible categories. The FAQ further states 'Nongovernmental entities and their representatives are NOT eligible to submit applications.'

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call offers up to USD 2,250,000 seed funding per proposal described as non-reimbursable grant funding through a Multi-Partner Trust Fund.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Funding is up to USD 2,250,000 per proposal, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The application deadline was 7 April 2025 at 23:59 EAT, which has passed relative to today's date of 2026-04-23.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for Expressions of Interest for project grants, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Co-financing is not stated as a mandatory requirement. Regional intergovernmental entity proposals that include non-ODA-eligible countries would need co-funding for activities in those countries, but this is a limited scenario rather than a general mandatory co-financing requirement.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required to apply.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call requires that applications be led by government entities. There is no specific rule about UN entities being excluded from consortium leadership beyond the general non-eligibility of non-governmental entities.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Applicants must be from countries or territories on the OECD DAC list of ODA Recipients, restricting eligibility to low- and middle-income countries as defined by the DAC.

results.title: Nature for Health (N4H) Phase II Expression of Interest
results.deadline_text: 7 April 2025 at 23:59 EAT (23:59 UTC + 3)
results.organization: Germany (Federal Ministry for the Environment, Nature Conservation, Nuclear Safety and Consumer Protection - BMUV, through IKI)
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies

application_process.description: Application window opens
Close of applications
Selection announced (August 2025)

eligible_activities: Country-led preventative policies and investments in nature to reduce health risks
Integration of environment into the One Health approach
Scoping phase activities (systemic approach workshops, stakeholder engagement, participatory identification of interventions) – approximately USD 250,000
Implementation of integrated, inclusive One Health approaches for nature and health
Multi-country or regional intergovernmental entity proposals on transboundary or landscape-based approaches
Activities addressing environmental determinants of health
Biodiversity and climate integration into One Health
Upstream prevention of zoonotic spillover
funding_restrictions: Non-governmental entities are not eligible to apply or receive direct funding
Under current funding rules, governments are not eligible to receive direct implementation funding (N4H partners receive funds and support implementation)
Funding cannot support activities in non-OECD DAC eligible countries (co-funding must be secured for such cases)
Countries that received funding through Phase I are not eligible
Maximum of one single-country proposal per country encouraged
results.proposal_summary: The Nature for Health (N4H) Phase II Expression of Interest, funded by Germany (BMUV/IKI) through a Multi-Partner Trust Fund with an initial contribution of €50 million, invites governments and regional intergovernmental entities from OECD DAC ODA-eligible countries to apply for seed funding of up to USD 2,250,000 per proposal to implement integrated, preventative One Health approaches addressing the environmental determinants of health, biodiversity loss, and zoonotic spillover risk. The call targets national, sub-national, and regional governmental entities across all low- and middle-income ODA-eligible countries; non-governmental entities are explicitly excluded. The application window opened on 10 January 2025 and closed on 7 April 2025, with selection expected in August 2025 and implementation spanning 2-3 years. Co-financing is not generally required but may be needed for activities in non-ODA-eligible countries within regional proposals.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses on nature-health interfaces, One Health approaches, biodiversity, and pandemic prevention—topics outside the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work—and ILO is explicitly ineligible as a non-governmental entity.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline passed: The submission deadline was 4 November 2024, which is before today's date of 2026-04-23. Additionally, the CfP is explicitly marked as CLOSED. While UN agencies (PUNOs) are eligible applicants, the call is closed.
results.classification_summary: This call for proposals is explicitly marked as CLOSED, with a submission deadline of 4 November 2024, which has passed relative to today's date of 2026-04-23. The ILO, as a UN agency, could potentially qualify as a PUNO (Participating UN Organization) if it signed the Fund's MOU, meaning the eligibility criterion is conditionally met. However, the expired deadline renders the opportunity ineligible regardless of other criteria. Grant sizes range from USD 100,000 to USD 500,000, which exceeds the threshold.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP explicitly lists UN agencies that are members of the Malaysia UN Country Team and recognized as PUNOs (by signing the Fund's MOU) as eligible applicants. ILO, as a UN agency, could qualify as a PUNO if it has signed the MOU, but this is conditional on MOU membership.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The Fund provides non-reimbursable grants disbursed via direct bank transfer in US Dollars to support SDG-related projects. No repayment obligation is mentioned.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Grant amounts range from a minimum of USD 100,000 to a maximum of USD 500,000 per proposal, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The submission deadline was 4 November 2024, 23:59 Malaysia Time, which has passed. The page title explicitly states 'CLOSED'.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for project proposals (grants), not a procurement or consultancy tender. No RFP, ITB, or consultant language is used.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Co-financing is not mentioned as a mandatory requirement. Multi-partner delivery is encouraged but not required as a cost-sharing condition.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: PUNOs must have signed the Fund's Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to be recognized as participating UN organizations eligible to apply. This constitutes a specific pre-existing accreditation requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No rule restricts consortium leadership to non-UN entities. PUNOs (UN agencies) can lead joint proposals involving at least two UN organizations, and may partner with non-UN entities.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: NUNOs must be registered in Malaysia. PUNOs must be members of the Malaysia UN Country Team. The fund targets projects in Malaysia only.

eligible_activities: Projects addressing poverty, nutritional and health challenges of marginal and excluded groups
Projects levelling up SDG performance in key human development domains across regions
Social protection measures for excluded and under-served groups and those in informal work
Addressing human development needs of women at risk of being left behind

results.proposal_summary: The Malaysia-UN SDG Trust Fund, a partnership between the United Nations in Malaysia and Yayasan MySDG (supported by Malaysia's Ministry of Finance), launched its Call for Proposals 2024/2025 to fund innovative, people-centric projects accelerating SDG progress in Malaysia, with a particular focus on marginalized and disadvantaged groups. The Fund supported projects under three thematic pillars: People & Prosperity, Planet, and Peace & Partnership, with grants ranging from USD 100,000 to USD 500,000 per proposal and project durations of 6 to 18 months. Eligible applicants included Malaysia-based CSOs, NGOs, academic institutions, and think-tanks (NUNOs), as well as UN agencies that are members of the Malaysia UN Country Team and have signed the Fund's MOU (PUNOs); social enterprises were explicitly excluded. The submission deadline was 4 November 2024, and this call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: Several priority areas align strongly with ILO's mandate, particularly those addressing informal sector workers, decent jobs, skills training, social protection for excluded groups, and labour rights for migrants and refugees, though the fund's broad SDG scope extends beyond the ILO's core focus areas.

With a total grant pool of EUR 1.8 million (PHP 125 million), the call aims to accelerate the circular and green transition of Filipino Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) in priority industries including agriculture and fisheries, tourism and hospitality, food, and textile. Eligible applicants are network-rooted actors (MSME business support networks and related private sector players) based in the Philippines. No co-financing requirement is explicitly stated. The ILO, as an international organization, is not an eligible applicant for this call.

results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through Internews International's FAIR Media Ukraine project, invites Ukrainian media outlets from eleven frontline regions (Donetsk, Luhansk, Kharkiv, Sumy, Chernihiv, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson, Mykolaiv, Odesa, Dnipropetrovsk, and Kyiv) to apply for grants averaging EUR 15,000 each for projects lasting up to eight months. The competition aims to support regional and local media operating under wartime conditions to continue delivering trusted, relevant information and strengthen their medium-term resilience. Eligible applicants must be media outlets registered in Ukraine that serve frontline audiences, demonstrate editorial independence, maintain an operational presence in their covered regions, and must not have previously received a FAIR Media Ukraine grant. The deadline for submission is 11 May 2026 at 18:00 Kyiv time. No co-financing is required. The ILO is not eligible as applicants are restricted to Ukrainian-registered media outlets, and the grant size is below the USD 50,000 threshold.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This grant focuses on media sustainability and wartime information provision in Ukraine's frontline regions, which does not align with the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The Delegation of the European Union to Uganda has launched a €5.1 million in-country Call for Proposals under the CSO Thematic Programme 2021-2027 (EuropeAid/186375/DD/ACT/Multi). The call targets Civil Society Organisations and aims to: (1) promote sustainable, inclusive and rights-based management of the environment and natural resources, focusing on forests, land use and related value chains; and (2) contribute to a more inclusive and equitable local economy in areas where the EU and Team Europe are engaged in Global Gateway investments in renewable energy, including through enhanced access to clean energy, decent work and diversified livelihoods in Uganda. The call is restricted to CSOs; international organizations such as the ILO are not eligible applicants. The deadline for submission of proposals is 4 June 2026, with an information session scheduled for 6 May 2026.

results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through a joint project of UNDP and the Anti-Corruption Agency of the Republic of Uzbekistan titled 'Strengthening the National Anti-Corruption Ecosystem in Uzbekistan', announces a small grants competition 'Civil Initiatives Against Corruption: Participation, Transparency and Solutions' targeting civil society organisations across all regions of Uzbekistan. The competition supports anti-corruption initiatives in four priority areas: civil society participation in law-making, digital transparency tools, women's integrity and economic empowerment, and local-level anti-corruption. The total competition budget is USD 30,000 and the maximum grant per project is USD 6,000 (paid in national currency). Implementation period is up to 6 months. Eligible applicants are exclusively national non-governmental non-profit organisations (including women's and youth organisations), and academic/research institutions registered and operating in Uzbekistan whose statutory objectives align with the competition theme. Commercial organisations, government bodies, organisations under liquidation, and individuals are not eligible. Applications must be submitted in Uzbek or Russian to anti-corruption.contest@undp.org by 00:00 Tashkent time on 4 May 2026. results.proposal_summary: The Adaptation Benefit Mechanism (ABM) is an initiative of the African Development Bank (AfDB) designed to mobilize public and private sector finance for climate change adaptation in Africa through a results-based finance model. The mechanism certifies social, economic, and environmental adaptation benefits and promotes these certificates to potential investors or lenders. Piloted from 2019 to 2023 across approximately 10-12 demonstration projects in Africa (now concluded), ABM covers a broad scope including resilient agriculture, coastal protection, clean energy, and weather information systems. This page is an informational overview of the mechanism and does not constitute an open Call for Proposals with a defined grant amount, eligible applicant categories, or submission deadline. The pilot phase has concluded and a permanent secretariat is being established by 2027.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The ABM focuses on climate change adaptation finance in Africa with some references to job creation for vulnerable groups and women's economic empowerment, which tangentially aligns with ILO's just transitions and decent work mandates, but the mechanism is primarily an environmental finance tool with no direct focus on labour standards, employment policy, or social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The ADF Climate Action Window is a dedicated financing facility established by the African Development Fund (ADF) under its 16th replenishment cycle to address the approximately $60 billion annual climate financing gap faced by low-income African countries. The Window allocates resources across three sub-windows: 75% for adaptation, 15% for mitigation, and 10% for technical assistance, covering six thematic sectors including agriculture and food security, water security, climate information and early warning, green transport and infrastructure, green energy and energy efficiency, and green finance. Eligible recipients are ADF-member low-income African countries (sub-Saharan Africa and other ADF-eligible states). The facility offers a large grant component to minimize debt distress risks. No specific submission deadline or maximum grant size is stated in the available text. As the programme targets sovereign governments rather than international organisations, the ILO as a UN agency would not qualify as an eligible applicant.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the ADF Climate Action Window touches on just transition themes (green energy, green infrastructure, adaptation), it is primarily a sovereign climate finance facility for African governments and does not align with the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or social dialogue.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Adaptation Acceleration Program (AAAP) is a joint initiative of the African Development Bank (AfDB) and the Global Center on Adaptation (GCA), aiming to mobilize $25 billion over five years to accelerate climate adaptation across Africa through four pillars: climate-smart digital agriculture (targeting 30 million farmers in 26 countries), infrastructure resilience, youth empowerment for green jobs (1 million youth), and innovative adaptation finance. The program operates through an AAAP Upstream Financing Facility at GCA and the ADF Climate Action Window providing up to $13 billion for low-income African countries. This page is an informational program description rather than a Call for Proposals; no submission deadline, eligible applicant categories, or specific grant size for external applicants are defined. The ILO contributed the 'Jobs' chapter to the associated State and Trends in Adaptation 2021 report, indicating potential thematic alignment.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AAAP focuses primarily on climate adaptation and resilience infrastructure finance in Africa; while one pillar addresses youth employment and green jobs (an ILO mandate area), the program is not a labour/decent work-focused CfP and does not constitute an open grant call for which ILO could apply.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Climate Change Fund (ACCF) is a multi-donor trust fund administered by the African Development Bank, established in 2014 with contributions from Germany, Belgium/Flanders, Italy, Canada, Austria, Ireland, the United States, the Province of Quebec, and the Global Center on Adaptation. The ACCF provides small grants to African governments, NGOs, and regional institutions to support Africa's transition towards climate-resilient, low-carbon development, with a focus on accessing climate finance, implementing NDCs, developing Long-Term Strategies, and undertaking small-scale adaptation and mitigation actions while promoting gender equality. The fund covers all African Development Bank regional member countries. No specific open call for proposals deadline, minimum/maximum grant amounts, or application process are stated in the publicly available page content. ILO as an international organization is not among the eligible applicant categories explicitly listed.

theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Disaster Risk Financing Programme (ADRiFi), initiated by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in 2018, aims to enhance the resilience of African countries to climate shocks by improving disaster risk management and financing. The programme's Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF), established in 2021 and funded by the United Kingdom, Switzerland, Canada, Norway, the Netherlands, and the United States (~USD 96.7 million total), provides premium subsidies and capacity development support to AfDB Regional Member Countries (sovereign governments) across Africa. It is implemented in partnership with the African Risk Capacity (ARC) Group. All AfDB Regional Member Countries are eligible upon formal request to the Bank and meeting requirements for participation in the ARC regional risk pool. This is not an open Call for Proposals for external organizations; there is no defined application deadline or per-organization grant size. The ILO is not an eligible applicant under this programme framework.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While ADRiFi touches on livelihoods and food security for vulnerable rural workers (including women in agriculture), its core focus is on climate disaster risk financing and insurance for sovereign governments, which falls outside the ILO's primary mandate of labour standards, employment, and social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The African Development Bank launched the USD 1.5 billion African Emergency Food Production Facility in May 2022 in response to food supply disruptions caused by the Russia-Ukraine war. The initiative aims to provide certified seeds and fertilizers to 20 million smallholder farmers across Africa, targeting a production increase of 38 million tonnes of food within two years. The facility is a sovereign lending and investment instrument directed at African member states and farmers; it is not an open call for proposals from NGOs, international organizations, or other external entities. No eligibility criteria for external applicants, no submission deadline, and no grant window for third parties are defined.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The facility addresses food security and agricultural resilience in Africa, which is tangentially related to ILO themes of decent work in crisis situations and rural employment, but its core focus is agricultural production finance rather than labour standards, social protection, or employment policy.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Fertilizer Financing Mechanism (AFFM) is a special fund administered by the African Development Bank Group, established by the African Union in 2006 to address low fertilizer use and stagnant agricultural productivity in Africa. The mechanism provides innovative financing solutions—primarily trade credit guarantees and grants—to accelerate fertilizer access and use in AfDB Regional Member Countries (RMCs) across the continent. The AFFM operates through a 2022–2028 strategic plan with three pillars: (i) strengthening the fertilizer sector through access to finance; (ii) supporting policy reforms; and (iii) facilitating smallholder farmers' access to inputs and technical assistance. Active projects have been implemented in Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Nigeria. The 2024 work program budget totals USD 18.58 million, including USD 1.20 million for new activities. This is not an open, competitive Call for Proposals; it is an institutional program page describing an ongoing AfDB financing mechanism. No submission deadline or open application process for external organizations (such as the ILO) exists. The mechanism is ineligible for ILO participation as it targets African national implementing partners, financial institutions, and agro-dealers in RMCs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AFFM focuses on agricultural fertilizer financing and food security in Africa, which has minimal direct alignment with ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work, though there is a very marginal connection through rural employment and smallholder farmer livelihoods.

results.proposal_summary: The African Green Banks Initiative (AGBI) is a pan-African initiative led by the African Development Bank, launched at COP27, in partnership with the Climate Investment Funds, Canada Climate Action, the Green Bank Network, Amundi, and the Multilateral Cooperation Center for Development Finance (MCDF). Its objective is to create an ecosystem of Green Banks across Africa by mobilising additional climate finance for green and sustainable growth aligned with countries' Nationally Determined Contributions. The initiative targets a $1.5 billion ecosystem of green investment facilities by 2030 through a blended finance approach comprising Technical Assistance and a Green Finance Facility. It is active across all African countries. This page is an informational overview — it does not constitute a Call for Proposals and provides no grant application process, eligible applicant list, or submission deadline.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AGBI focuses on climate finance mobilisation and green banking in Africa, which has only tangential relevance to ILO's mandate on decent work and just transitions, but does not directly address labour standards, employment, or social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Trade Fund (AfTra) is a multi-million dollar trade-related technical assistance facility established by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in March 2012, with seed financing from the Government of Canada. Its objective is to accelerate the integration of African countries and regional economic communities (RECs) into regional and global trading systems, complementing the Bank's operations in agriculture, infrastructure, governance, private sector development, and science and technology. The fund covers all African Development Bank member countries across the continent. No active Call for Proposals, submission deadline, per-grant funding amount, or applicant eligibility criteria are currently specified on the fund's webpage, making this an informational/program overview page rather than an open funding opportunity as of April 2026.

results.proposal_summary: The African Beneficial Ownership Transparency Network (AfBOTN) is an initiative launched in 2023 by the African Development Bank in partnership with the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) and other partners. Its purpose is to bring together international partners and African governments to support regional priorities in driving reforms around beneficial ownership transparency across Africa, combating illicit financial flows, money laundering, and corruption. The network focuses on peer learning, information sharing, and practical guidance for implementing beneficial ownership registers across African countries. This page is an informational description of the network initiative and does not constitute a Call for Proposals; no grant funding, application deadline, eligible applicant categories, or grant amounts are specified.

results.proposal_summary: The UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) launched the Education for All South Sudan (EFASS) grant programme targeting the most vulnerable out-of-school children affected by climate and conflict shocks in South Sudan. With a grant of up to £27 million over four fiscal years (April 2026 to March 2030), EFASS shifts from the nationwide delivery model of its predecessor to a hybrid, targeted approach focusing on girls at risk of dropping out, children with disabilities, and the most underserved communities. Eligible applicants were NGOs, UK-based non-profits, humanitarian relief organisations, and LMIC-based organisations registered and operationally present in South Sudan. results.proposal_summary: UK Aid Match is a grant programme administered by the UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO), implemented through fund manager MannionDaniels, providing match-funding grants to UK-based civil society organisations, NGOs, and humanitarian relief organisations for poverty reduction projects in developing countries across Africa, Asia, the Middle East, and beyond. For every £1 raised by a UK charity from the British public, the UK government contributes £1 of UK aid. Grants range from over £100,000 to more than £1,000,000 GBP. Eligible sectors include health, education, WASH, livelihoods, climate change, disabilities, and humanitarian response. Applicants must be UK-based non-profit or civil society organisations; international organizations such as the ILO are not eligible. No current round deadline is specified on the page; applications are made via www.ukaidmatch.org. The programme also supports Disasters Emergency Committee appeals at times of humanitarian crisis.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the programme funds livelihoods, social protection, and development projects broadly aligned with decent work themes, it is specifically directed at UK-based civil society charities for poverty reduction and humanitarian response, with limited focus on ILO core mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, social dialogue, or just transitions.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) 2026 for Guyana and Suriname, administered by Global Affairs Canada, supports small-scale, high-impact local development projects aligned with Canada's thematic priorities including inclusive growth, democratic governance, and peace and security. Eligible recipients include local and international NGOs, intergovernmental and multilateral organizations (including the ILO, provided it works with local partners on local projects), academic institutions, Canadian NGOs, and government institutions of the recipient countries. The maximum grant per project is CAD$100,000 (average CAD$50,000), provided as a non-reimbursable contribution. Projects must be completed by February 26, 2027 (single-year) or February 25, 2028 (two-year), covering the period from April 2026. Completed applications using the designated form must be submitted by email to GRGTNG@international.gc.ca by April 30, 2026 at 23:59 GMT-4. No co-financing requirement or specific accreditation is mandated.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CFLI covers some themes relevant to ILO's mandate (e.g., technical/vocational training and inclusive markets in Guyana), the primary focus is on peace and security, anti-trafficking, and democratic governance rather than core ILO priorities such as labour standards, social protection, or social dialogue, and the small grant size limits strategic alignment.

results.exclusion_reason: Grant size below USD 50,000 threshold: maximum allocation is CAD 100,000, which falls below the USD 50,000 threshold only when considering that average grants are CAD 15,000–30,000; additionally, the eligible applicant list is exhaustive and does not clearly include the ILO as an international organization in the way the program prioritizes local civil society.
results.classification_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – Cambodia is a small-grant program administered by Global Affairs Canada targeting local civil society and development projects in Cambodia. The maximum grant per project is CAD 100,000, which at current exchange rates is below the USD 50,000 threshold required for ILO eligibility. While intergovernmental and multilateral organizations are listed as potentially eligible, they must work with local partners on local projects, and the program's primary focus is local civil society organizations. The grant size criterion is the decisive exclusion factor.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP explicitly lists 'Intergovernmental, multilateral and regional institutions, organizations and agencies working on local development activities' as eligible recipients, which could encompass the ILO as a UN/intergovernmental organization, provided it works with local partners on local projects.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The program provides non-reimbursable contributions (grants) described as 'CFLI contribution' with no mention of repayment obligations.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The maximum allocation per project is CAD 100,000, with average contributions of CAD 15,000–30,000. At current exchange rates, CAD 100,000 is approximately USD 72,000–75,000, which is above USD 50,000; however, the average grant of CAD 15,000–30,000 is well below USD 50,000. Since the maximum stated is CAD 100,000 (~USD 72,000), strictly the maximum exceeds the threshold. Re-evaluating: the maximum of CAD 100,000 is above USD 50,000, so this criterion passes on its face.

Deadline is future.evidence: The page content is truncated and no specific submission deadline date is explicitly stated in the provided text. The program is described as the 2026 cycle but no closing date is given in the available content.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant program for development projects, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing requirement is mentioned in the available text.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the available text.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The program is directed primarily at local civil society organizations in Cambodia and international organizations working on local Cambodian projects; Canadian NGOs are also eligible but the geographic focus is Cambodia-based or Cambodia-focused activities.

Grants range from an average of CAD 15,000–30,000 up to a maximum of CAD 100,000 per project. Eligible applicants include local NGOs, community and not-for-profit organizations, local academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental and multilateral organizations (working with local partners on local projects), municipal/regional/national government institutions of Cambodia, and Canadian NGOs. Projects must be locally conceived and designed, and applications must be submitted using the designated form in English or French. The deadline for the 2026 cycle is not stated in the available content. While intergovernmental organizations such as the ILO are technically listed as potentially eligible, the program's primary focus is local civil society, and the grant amounts are modest.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The CFLI Cambodia program supports broad local development activities without a specific focus on labour standards, employment, or social protection, making it a weak match for ILO's core mandate despite some thematic overlap possible in project design.

results.exclusion_reason: Grant size below threshold: maximum allocation is CAD 100,000 (~USD 72,000) but the average contribution is CAD 20,000–40,000, which at current exchange rates is well below USD 50,000. Additionally, the ILO Eligibility criterion is borderline: while intergovernmental organizations are listed as eligible, they must work with local partners on local projects, and the thematic priorities (governance, peace/security, women's economic rights) do not align closely with ILO's core mandate. The grant size is the primary exclusion trigger.
results.classification_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – ASEAN 2026 is a small-grant programme administered by Global Affairs Canada, targeting small-scale, high-impact projects across ASEAN countries. Eligible recipients include local NGOs, academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental organizations, and government institutions working at the local level. The maximum grant is CAD 100,000 (average CAD 20,000–40,000), which converts to approximately USD 73,000 at most (below average is well under USD 50,000). Thematic priorities are inclusive governance, peace and security, and inclusive growth/women's economic security. The deadline is April 30, 2026, which is in the future. However, the typical grant size falls well below USD 50,000, triggering the grant size exclusion criterion.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP lists eligible recipients including 'Intergovernmental, multilateral and regional institutions, organizations and agencies working on local development activities.' The ILO, as an intergovernmental organization, could technically fit this category provided it works with local partners on local projects. However, eligibility is conditional on working locally, which may be limiting.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CFLI is described as a contribution/grant programme providing non-reimbursable funding to eligible recipients. The text refers to 'CFLI contribution' and 'Contribution Agreement,' confirming this is a non-repayable grant.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The maximum allocation is CAD 100,000 (approximately USD 73,000 at current rates), but the average contribution is CAD 20,000–40,000 (approximately USD 14,700–29,400), which is well below the USD 50,000 threshold. Even the maximum CAD 100,000 is only marginally above USD 50,000 and the programme is explicitly designed for small-scale projects averaging far below threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submission is April 30, 2026, at 23:59 (GMT+7), which is after today's date of April 22, 2026.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant programme for project proposals, not a procurement or consultancy tender. No RFP, ITB, or consultant language is used.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing or cost-sharing requirement is mentioned in the CfP. The text does not make co-funding a mandatory condition.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g., GEF, Green Climate Fund) is required. Applicants simply need to use the designated application form.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned. The CfP does not specify that partnerships must be led by a non-UN entity.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Applicants are not restricted to a specific country of registration. The CfP targets local organizations in ASEAN countries as well as international NGOs, intergovernmental organizations, and Canadian NGOs working locally.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – ASEAN 2026, administered by Global Affairs Canada, provides small-scale non-reimbursable grants of CAD 20,000–100,000 to support high-impact projects across ASEAN member states (Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam). Projects must impact at least three ASEAN countries and align with thematic priorities including inclusive governance, peace and security, and growth that works for everyone (with emphasis on women's economic rights). Priority sub-themes include AI governance, Women, Peace and Security, democratic governance, youth and peacebuilding, and maritime cooperation. Eligible applicants include local NGOs, academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental organizations, and government institutions working locally, as well as Canadian not-for-profit organizations. Projects must be completed by February 26, 2027. Applications must be submitted by April 30, 2026 at 23:59 GMT+7 to aseancanadacfli@gmail.com. No co-financing requirement is mandated.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CfP includes a sub-theme on women's economic rights and economic security that partially aligns with ILO's decent work mandate, its primary thematic focus is on governance, peace and security, and AI governance, which falls outside ILO's core areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, and social dialogue.

It supports small-scale, high-impact development projects in Angola conceived and designed predominantly by local partners and aligned with Canada's international development thematic priorities. results.ilo_match_evidence: The CFLI Angola call focuses on small local development projects broadly aligned with Global Affairs Canada priorities; while it may touch on employment or social themes tangentially, it is not specifically oriented toward ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, decent work, or social protection.

The more decisive exclusion is the deadline: the first mandatory submission stage opens 11 August 2025 and closes 15 September 2025, both of which are in the future relative to today (2026-04-23) — wait, actually the deadline of 15 September 2025 is BEFORE today (2026-04-23), so the deadline has passed.

results.title: RISE Grants Challenge 2025 – Resilient, Inclusive and Sustainable Environments
results.deadline_text: 15 September 2025 – Application submission deadline – late submissions will not be considered.
results.organization: Norway / Norwegian Agency for Development Cooperation (Norad)
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies

application_process.description: Call for Proposals launched; question submission window opens
Application window opens via SMApply
Deadline for submitting questions via email to RISE@iucn.org
Frequently Asked Questions published online
Proposal submission deadline (Stage 1)
Eligibility and technical screening completed (Stage 2)
Technical Evaluation Committee review and scoring (Stage 3)
Applicant interviews by RISE Steering Committee
Due diligence finalists selected (Stage 4)
Grant agreements signed; all applicants informed (Stage 5)

eligible_activities: Gender-based violence risk mitigation embedded in environmental projects
Gender analysis to assess context and inform GBV risk mitigation
Baseline and endline assessments to measure changes in perceptions of safety
Coordination with existing GBV referral pathways and service providers
Engaging men and boys as a risk mitigation strategy
Women's economic empowerment and capacity building activities
Evidence-based strategies adapted from global GBV approaches for environment-focused sectors
Survivor-centred and trauma-informed approaches
Strengthening safeguards in environmental programmes
Training environmental stakeholders on GBV risk mitigation
Institutional learning to improve rights-based gender-responsive environment approaches
Innovative arrangements via partnerships, policy reform or social protection measures
Enabling conditions (legislative action, infrastructure, institutionalisation)
Bridging knowledge gaps and demonstrating context-specific promising practices
Self- and collective care structures for staff well-being
funding_restrictions: Projects must be implemented in one of the 23 listed eligible countries only
Governments and public entities are not eligible as partners (only as stakeholders)
Publicly funded universities and academic institutions are not eligible
For-profit organisations must demonstrate strictly non-profit project objectives with no income generation
Proposals working outside marine/terrestrial Indigenous-managed lands, protected areas, or OECMs are out of scope
Proposals focusing on GBV prevention and response only (without risk mitigation focus) are not prioritised
Lead applicants must partner with no more than two organisations
Proposals must be submitted in English only; hand-written documents not accepted
results.proposal_summary: The RISE Grants Challenge 2025, led by IUCN with funding from Norway and Norad, is a sixth-cycle open call supporting projects that address gender-based violence (GBV) risk mitigation within environmental and climate-related programmes, specifically in marine and/or terrestrial Indigenous-managed lands, protected areas, and OECMs. Eligible applicants include NGOs, CBOs, CSOs, IGOs, INGOs, research institutes, and others legally registered locally, nationally, or internationally, operating in one of 23 eligible countries across Africa, Asia, and Latin America. Grant amounts are not specified. The proposal submission deadline was 15 September 2025 (now passed). No mandatory co-financing is required. Applications must be in English and submitted via SMApply.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The RISE call focuses primarily on gender-based violence and environment/conservation linkages rather than ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or social dialogue, though intersectional elements such as women's economic empowerment and social protection measures have marginal relevance.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO (as a UN agency/international organization) is excluded by the ineligible applicants provisions. The CfP lists eligible applicants but Section 4.8 references ineligible applicants, and the deadline for the application window (08 September 2025) has not yet passed as of today (2026-04-23) — however, the application window opened 11 August 2025 and closed 08 September 2025, which is before today's date, making the deadline past. Additionally, the CfP's eligible applicant list includes NGOs, IGOs, CBOs, CSOs, IPOs, universities, research institutes, and private sector companies, but the context and Section 4.8 on ineligible applicants (not fully reproduced) and the requirement that at least one lead/partner organisation must be an IUCN member organisation, past RISE grantee, or past/present EbA Fund grantee effectively restricts eligibility. The submission deadline of 08 September 2025 has passed.
results.classification_summary: The application deadline was 08 September 2025, which has passed as of today (2026-04-23), rendering this CfP ineligible on the deadline criterion. Furthermore, the CfP lists eligible applicant categories (NGOs, IGOs, CBOs, CSOs, IPOs, universities, research institutes, private sector companies) with a specific requirement that at least one lead or partner organisation must be an IUCN member organisation, a past RISE grantee, or a past/present EbA Fund grantee — a condition the ILO does not meet. The grant is non-reimbursable and above USD 50,000, so those criteria are met, but the past deadline and eligibility restrictions exclude the ILO.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP lists eligible applicants including NGOs, IGOs, CBOs, CSOs, IPOs, universities, research institutes, and private sector companies, and requires at least one lead or partner organisation to be an IUCN member organisation, past RISE grantee, or past/present EbA Fund grantee. The ILO does not qualify under these specific conditions and is not listed as an eligible category in any straightforward manner that would include a UN agency.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CfP explicitly offers grants of USD 100,000–300,000 (non-reimbursable) under the RISE grants challenge mechanism.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Grant amounts range from USD 100,000 to USD 300,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The application submission deadline was 08 September 2025, which has passed as of today (2026-04-23).

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The opportunity is framed as a grants challenge call for proposals, not a procurement, RFP, or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Section 6.2 references 'Cost-share' as a component of the awards, suggesting co-financing may be required. The CfP document includes a dedicated cost-share section, indicating it is a requirement rather than merely optional.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with a body such as GEF or Green Climate Fund is required; however, a partnership requirement with an IUCN member organisation or past grantee exists.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No rule explicitly prohibiting UN entities from leading consortia is stated; the consortium/partnership requirements relate to IUCN membership or past grantee status.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Eligibility is open to local, national, regional, or international organisations across 23 targeted countries; no restriction to donor domestic territory only.

eligible_activities: Strategies to prevent, mitigate and respond to gender-based violence linked to environmental contexts

Activities generating evidence on promising interventions at the GBV-environment nexus

results.proposal_summary: The RISE (Resilient, Inclusive and Sustainable Environments) grants challenge 2025 Call for Proposals, managed by IUCN with support from Norway/Norad, seeks projects that embed gender-based violence (GBV) risk mitigation strategies within environmental and climate-related programmes, with a specific focus on biodiversity and climate contexts and a special earmark for the fisheries sector. Grants of USD 100,000–300,000 are available for up to three awards, with implementation periods of 12–18 months, targeting 23 eligible countries. Eligible applicants include NGOs, IGOs, CBOs, CSOs, IPOs, universities, research institutes, and private sector companies, with a mandatory requirement that at least one lead or partner organisation is an IUCN member organisation, past RISE grantee, or past/present EbA Fund grantee. The application deadline was 08 September 2025, which has passed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CfP addresses gender equality and the rights of women workers in environmental sectors (including fisheries), its primary focus is on gender-based violence in conservation and environmental contexts rather than on core ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or social dialogue.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not among the eligible applicants. The CfP explicitly restricts eligibility to accredited multilateral development banks (MDBs) that are We-Fi Implementing Partners: African Development Bank, Asian Development Bank, EBRD, Inter-American Development Bank, IDB Invest, IFC, Islamic Development Bank Group, and World Bank. The ILO is not listed.
results.classification_summary: The CfP is ineligible for the ILO because the eligibility section explicitly and exhaustively restricts applicants to eight named accredited multilateral development banks that are We-Fi Implementing Partners; the ILO is not among them. The funding instrument is a grant (non-reimbursable, minimum USD 2.5 million) and the deadline of September 15, 2025 is in the future relative to today, but ILO eligibility fails as a hard exclusion criterion.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The Eligibility section explicitly lists only eight accredited MDBs as eligible applicants (African Development Bank, Asian Development Bank, EBRD, Inter-American Development Bank, IDB Invest, IFC, Islamic Development Bank Group, and World Bank). The ILO is not included in this exhaustive list.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: We-Fi provides non-reimbursable grant funding to Implementing Partners; the call states 'at least USD 15 million of financing is available' with a minimum threshold of USD 2.5 million per proposal, with no repayment obligation mentioned.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The minimum funding request per proposal is USD 2.5 million, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: Deadline 2025-09-15 is before today (2026-04-23).

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is framed as a grant call for proposals, not a procurement or consultancy tender. No RFP or ITB language is used.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The Funding Principles state that proposals must demonstrate 'catalyzing co-funding from other sources,' indicating co-financing is a required element of strong proposals.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: Applicants must be 'accredited' MDBs that are We-Fi Implementing Partners, making pre-existing accreditation with We-Fi a mandatory condition.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the CfP text.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Eligibility is restricted by institution type (accredited MDB Implementing Partners), not by the domestic registration country of the applicant.

results.proposal_summary: The Women Entrepreneurs Finance Initiative (We-Fi), housed at the World Bank, has launched its 6th Call for Proposals with at least USD 15 million available to address financial and non-financial constraints faced by women-owned/led SMEs in IDA/IBRD-eligible and ODA-eligible countries. This round focuses specifically on the care economy, seeking to support women-led businesses offering innovative digital technologies and creative solutions to care needs, as well as improving access to finance, training, and skills for care economy businesses. Eligible applicants are exclusively the eight accredited MDB Implementing Partners (African Development Bank, Asian Development Bank, EBRD, Inter-American Development Bank, IDB Invest, IFC, Islamic Development Bank Group, and World Bank). The minimum funding request per proposal is USD 2.5 million, with a strategic priority for IDA and fragile/conflict-affected countries. Co-funding mobilization is a required funding principle. Proposals must be submitted via email by 23:59 EST on September 15, 2025. The ILO is not eligible as it is not among the listed Implementing Partners.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The CfP targets women's economic empowerment, employment, job creation, and skills development in the care economy—areas closely aligned with ILO's decent work agenda—but eligibility is restricted to accredited MDB Implementing Partners, excluding the ILO.

results.proposal_summary: The IKI Large Grants Call 2025 (ILG Call 2025) is funded by the German Federal Ministry for the Environment, Climate Action, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety through the International Climate Initiative (IKI), administered by ZUG gGmbH. The call provided grants of EUR 12–20 million per project for international implementing organisations to address climate change mitigation, adaptation, and biodiversity conservation across specific country priorities (Mexico, India, South Africa) and thematic priorities including energy transition, bioeconomy, coastal resilience in South/Southeast Asia, and decarbonisation in Sub-Saharan Africa and South/Southeast Asia. The submission deadline for project outlines was 17 February 2026 at 2 pm CET, which has now passed. A total of 501 outlines were received. No co-financing requirement or specific accreditation is stated in the available text. The call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses primarily on climate change mitigation, biodiversity conservation, and ecosystem adaptation, with no explicit focus on labour standards, decent work, employment, social protection, or social dialogue that characterise the ILO mandate.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The funding announcement text does not explicitly state whether co-financing is mandatory within the extracted content.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g., GEF, GCF) is required.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The concept note template mentions implementing organisations and partners, but no explicit rule requiring consortium leadership by a non-UN entity is stated in the extracted text.
